title: Collective amplification and anisotropic narrowing of alignment signals in cesium vapor under strong spin exchange near zero magnetic field
authors: Mikhail V. Petrenko, Anton K. Vershovskii
abstract: We present the results of an experimental study of the anomalous anisotropy of alignment signals in cesium vapors under strong spin exchange conditions in zero magnetic fields under linearly polarized optical pumping. We show that the anisotropy of the Hanle resonances in the plane perpendicular to the pump beam increases sharply with increasing concentration. In one direction, the resonance widths are determined by classical spin exchange, while in the other, by the SERF (Spin-Exchange Relaxation Free) effect. With further concentration increases, additional nonlinear effects arise, such as an increase of the normalized signal amplitude, effective magnetic field, bistability, hysteresis, and memory. To explain these observations, as well as the results presented in our previous studies, we construct a demonstration theoretical model incorporating spontaneous polarization effects arising under strong spin exchange. The model qualitatively shows that the experimentally observed ultra-narrow alignment resonances may originate predominantly from quadrupole anisotropy associated with spontaneous transverse orientation projected onto the detection axis.The unique properties of these resonances, such as their ultra-small width and magnetic field-controlled bistability with a long-term memory effect, make them promising for use in quantum sensing and information.
summary: The paper investigates unexpected signal narrowing and nonlinear effects in cesium vapor under strong spin-exchange conditions. It demonstrates that these phenomena, including bistability and memory, stem from the spontaneous polarization of atoms. This discovery suggests a way to use atomic vapors as amplifiers for high-precision quantum sensing.
main_problem: Investigating the origin of anomalous anisotropy, ultra-narrow resonance widths, and nonlinear phenomena like bistability and hysteresis in cesium vapor under strong spin-exchange conditions.
main_result: The study identifies that ultra-narrow alignment resonances arise from the projection of spontaneous transverse orientation onto the detection axis, effectively acting as an amplifier for alignment signals.
method: Experimental 2D magnetic field mapping and polarization rotation measurements in Cs vapor, complemented by a theoretical model incorporating spontaneous polarization and nonlinear dynamics.
model_or_system: Cesium (Cs) vapor in a nitrogen buffer gas at high pressure, operating in the Spin-Exchange Relaxation-Free (SERF) regime under linearly polarized optical pumping.
key_observables: Polarization rotation signal (Sb), transmission signal (St), alignment (quadrupole moment), and orientation (dipole moment).
important_parameters: Spin-exchange rate (Rse), Larmor frequency, temperature (77-103 C), and magnetic field components (Bx, By, Bz).
main_assumptions: The theoretical model is a minimal version using only the lowest relevant tensor moments and treats spontaneous polarization as a coherent process without additional dissipation.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the experimental setup; Figure 2 displays polarization rotation signals at different temperatures; Figure 3 shows transmission signals; Figure 6 illustrates the formation of alignment components.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the anomalous anisotropy, describes the experimental setup and high-density regime, presents the observed nonlinearities (bistability/hystricsis), proposes a theoretical model based on spontaneous polarization, and concludes with the geometric interpretation of the signals.
why_interesting: It explores spontaneous symmetry breaking and nonlinear collective dynamics in an open quantum system, providing a geometric mechanism for signal amplification and ultra-narrow resonances relevant to quantum sensing.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. (a) The experimental setup: MS - magnetic shield, LP - linear polarizer (Glan prism), HC - Helmholtz coils sys- tem, C - cell, PBS - polarization cube, PHD1, PHD2 - pho- todiodes.

Figure 2. Signals SB during magnetic field scanning depending on Bx, By at four temperatures. (a)–(d) Experiment, scanning direction is along x. (e)–(h) Modified theory Eq. (1). Modifications applied to Eqs. (1) of theoretical expressions are described in the text, k = Γy/Γx. White lines in fragments (e)–(h) indicate approximated dependences of the extrema positions.

Figure 3. Signals ST during magnetic field scanning depending on Bx, By at four temperatures. (a)–(d) Experiment, scanning direction is along x. (e)–(h) Modified theory Eq. (2). Modifications applied to Eqs. (2) of theoretical expressions are described in the text. The signal in fragment (a) is indistinguishable among the noise.

Figure 4. Sb signals. (a) Results of scanning from the edge of a circle with a radius of 42.5 nT to the center. (b) Results of scanning from the upper semicircle to the lower one. The interval between recordings was 5◦. The same data set was used in fragments (a) and (b).

Figure 5. (a), (b), (c) Signals selected from the same data array as Fig. 4. (a) ST . (b), (c) SB. Solid lines present scanning from negative Bx to positive, dots – vice versa. (d) Dependences of signal amplitudes on the By field at two temperatures – below and above the threshold. Dotted and dash-dotted lines are dispersion curves, solid line is modification of the dispersion curve according to Eq. (3) at By0 = 23 nT.
